content:
I thought AI would make me a faster programmer. Instead, it exposed every weakness in how I write software.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Growtika on Unsplashread here.
The first week I started using Claude Code seriously, I made the same mistake most developers make with AI.
I treated it like a smarter autocomplete.
I’d throw in a vague prompt, wait a few seconds, copy the output, and move on. It worked — until the projects became larger. Then something interesting happened.
Claude Code wasn’t slowing me down.
It was exposing every bad habit I’d quietly built over the last few years.
Poor project organization.
Functions doing ten different jobs.
Variables with meaningless names.
Files that nobody — including future me — wanted to open again.
The uncomfortable truth is this:
AI doesn’t magically fix messy engineering. It magnifies it.
Once I stopped asking, “How can AI write more code for me?” and started asking, “How can I become someone AI can collaborate with?” everything changed.
